title: NOC 65211 – Operators and attendants in amusement, recreation and sport | GTR Immigration
description: Operators and attendants under NOC 65211 are the heartbeat of Canada's vibrant entertainment and leisure sectors. From operating high-tech theme park rides and managing ski lifts to supervising sports facilities and water slides, these TEER 5 professionals ensure the safety and enjoyment of millions of visitors. In 2026, as Canada expands its "Experience Economy" and prepares for major global sporting events, this role serves as a vital entry point for international workers. For immigration, this occupation is a key component of Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs) and the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP), particularly in tourism-heavy regions.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category6 – Sales and service occupationsTEER Level5Major Group65 – Sales and service support occupationsSub-Major Group652 – Support occupations in food, accommodation and tourismMinor Group6521 – Accommodation and travel support occupationsNOC Code65211

duties: Set up, operate, and take down amusement rides and equipment.
Assist patrons on and off rides or in the use of recreational equipment.
Monitor activities to ensure the safety of patrons and ensure that safety regulations are followed.
Collect tickets and admit patrons to amusement parks, ski centers, or sports facilities.
Perform routine maintenance and minor repairs on rides and equipment.
Rent out sports or recreational equipment and maintain records of rentals.
Clean and maintain recreational facilities, including pools, change rooms, and sports courts.
employment_requirements: No formal education is usually required, though some secondary school is often preferred.
On-the-job training is provided for all specific safety and equipment operations.
First Aid/CPR Certification is often a mandatory requirement for safety-sensitive roles.
Basic proficiency in English or French is essential for patron safety and interaction.
Physical stamina and the ability to work outdoors in various weather conditions are required for many roles.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate a steady increase in job openings as the tourism sector reaches new capacity levels. As Canada invests in year-round recreation infrastructure (such as climate-controlled indoor sports hubs), the need for facility attendants will move beyond purely seasonal demand, providing more stable year-round employment opportunities.
labour_market_demand: The demand for amusement and recreation attendants is Very Good, specifically in high-growth tourism corridors. In 2026, there is a noted surge in demand in Southern Ontario (Niagara Falls/Toronto), The Rockies (Banff/Whistler), and Atlantic Canada. Employers are increasingly utilizing the Temporary Foreign Worker Program (TFWP) and LMIA pathways to staff seasonal and year-round recreation facilities.
High Accessibility: Minimal formal education requirements allow you to secure employment and begin your Canadian journey quickly.
Dynamic Environments: Work in some of Canada’s most beautiful and exciting locations, from world-class ski resorts to massive indoor waterparks.
Path to Supervision: Excellent stepping stone to become a Recreation & Sport Program Supervisor (NOC 52120) or Facility Manager.
Regional PR Priority: Targeted by specialized immigration streams in provinces looking to bolster their tourism infrastructure.
pathways: BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): A highly popular route for those working in tourism and recreation for at least 9 months in BC.
Alberta Tourism and Hospitality Stream: A dedicated pathway for workers in eligible leisure and hospitality occupations.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A direct route for workers with job offers in the Maritimes (NS, NB, PEI, NL).
Manitoba Skilled Worker Stream: Accessible for those who have worked in the province for at least 6 months.
